policy_statement: In forestry and other land uses, Indonesia identifies actions required to reduce emissions and achieve net sink (2050 under CPOS; 2030 under LCCP), including reducing deforestation/peat emissions, increasing sequestration via reduced degradation and enhanced regeneration, peatland restoration, forest restoration/sink enhancement, sustainable forest management, and maximizing use of unproductive lands.
mechanism_description: Indonesia plans to reduce emissions from deforestation and peat decomposition/fires and increase carbon sequestration through reduced forest degradation, enhanced regeneration, peatland and forest restoration, sustainable forest management, and using idle lands for forest/plantation establishment.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration → ↑ carbon storage & sequestration + ↓ land-use emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Protect and restore forests and peatlands via reduced deforestation/degradation, improved sustainable forest management, assisted natural regeneration, peat rewetting/restoration, and establishment of forests/plantations on idle/unproductive lands.  
(2) Mediating process: Maintaining/expanding biomass and soil carbon stocks; rewetting peat reduces aerobic decomposition and lowers fire susceptibility; restored vegetation increases net primary productivity and carbon uptake.  
(3) Climate outcome: Lower net land-sector GHG emissions (avoided CO₂ from biomass/soil loss, avoided peat CO₂/CH₄ and fire emissions) and increased long-term carbon sequestration (enhanced sink strength).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions by avoiding CO₂/CH₄/N₂O releases from deforestation and peatland degradation/fires while increasing carbon sequestration through ecosystem restoration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration primarily affects climate by preserving and increasing terrestrial carbon stocks and reducing land-use emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While outcomes can include adaptation co-benefits (flood regulation, heat buffering, fire risk reduction) and resource-efficiency elements (better land productivity), the core and largest climate effect across all such policies is biogenic carbon preservation/sequestration and avoided LULUCF emissions—i.e., a Nature-Based Solutions pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance emphasized plantations for timber supply or efficiency rather than ecosystem carbon/sinks, classification could shift; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly centers on forest/peat protection/restoration and carbon outcomes, keeping NBS dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that protects/restores forests/peatlands to avoid emissions and enhance sequestration operates through ecosystem carbon dynamics as the primary pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy instance is primarily “avoidance” (protection) vs “removal” (restoration/sequestration), and whether adaptation co-benefits are material, must be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and emphasis.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may include monoculture plantations on “idle” lands; if these primarily function as industrial forestry with limited ecosystem integrity or if land conversion risks biodiversity/soil carbon losses, the net effect and categorization could require policy-level review. Peat restoration often has strong mitigation dominance, but also significant adaptation/fire-risk benefits that remain secondary under this mechanism.
key_indicators: “turn it into net sink by 2050… and by 2030”; “depends primarily on the success of the following actions”; “reducing emission from deforestation and peatland”; “restoring peatland”; “implementing forest restoration”; “adopting sustainable forest management practices”; “increasing the capacity… in sequestering carbon”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; watershed/soil protection; reduced wildfire smoke/air pollution; improved rural livelihoods/land productivity.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although several listed actions could be implemented via mandates or investments (e.g., restoration programs, peat rewetting requirements), the excerpt itself does not specify binding requirements, budgets, or enforcement—so it is classified as a planning/strategy statement with targets rather than a regulatory or investment instrument.
